8.text: The CLI imports BedrockTokenCounter directly at module level (unlike the SDK's deferred import). This is fine because the CLI module is only loaded when the user runs the command — there's no circular import risk here.

9.text: Three specific botocore exceptions are imported for granular error handling. Each maps to a different user-facing message: missing credentials, missing region, or API-level failures. This is the boundary where AWS internals get translated into actionable CLI output.

12.text: The context_settings enables both -h and --help. Click only supports --help by default — adding -h matches the convention users expect from Unix tools and the original ttok.

14.text: nargs=-1 accepts any number of positional arguments and joins them as the input text. This matches ttok's behavior: ttok4bedrock one two three counts tokens for "one two three".

17.text: The -t flag for truncation is the key feature beyond simple counting. It triggers the adaptive truncation algorithm in BedrockTokenCounter.truncate(), which minimizes API calls while finding the exact token boundary.

19.text: The -m flag uses the same default model as the SDK. Keeping defaults consistent across CLI and SDK prevents subtle behavior differences that would confuse users switching between the two interfaces.

22.text: This is the main CLI function. Click's decorator stack above transforms it into a full command-line application with argument parsing, help text generation, and version display — all from a single function definition.

66.text: Input assembly follows ttok's precedence: if no arguments and no -i file, read from stdin. This enables pipe-friendly usage like cat file.txt | ttok4bedrock.

73.text: When both file input and arguments are provided, file content comes first. This matches ttok's concatenation behavior and lets users combine sources: ttok4bedrock -i header.txt "extra text".

84.text: The counter is created inside the try block so that credential errors during client initialization are caught by the exception handlers below.

87.text: The two code paths diverge here: truncation outputs text (no trailing newline, for pipe compatibility), while counting outputs a number (with newline). This matches ttok's output contract exactly.

90.text: nl=False suppresses the trailing newline so truncated output can be piped cleanly: cat large.txt | ttok4bedrock -t 100 > truncated.txt. Adding a newline would corrupt the output.

97.text: The error handling cascade is ordered from most specific to least specific. NoCredentialsError is the most common first-time setup issue, so it gets the most helpful message.

105.text: NoRegionError is separate from credentials because the fix is different: users need --aws-region or AWS_DEFAULT_REGION, not aws configure. Specific error messages reduce support burden.

113.text: ClientError catches all remaining Bedrock API failures (invalid model, throttling, access denied). The error code and message are extracted from the boto3 response structure and formatted for readability.

125.text: The __main__ guard allows running the module directly with python -m ttok4bedrock.cli, but the primary entry point is the ttok4bedrock console script defined in pyproject.toml.
